page: Microsoft Azure D64nls_v6 is a network-optimized, low-memory virtual machine belonging to the Dnlv6 server family. Powered by the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor, this instance features 64 dedicated vCPUs mapped from 32 physical cores of an Intel Xeon 8573C processor. The system architecture is x86_64, with a thread-per-core ratio of 2.0. Memory capacity is configured at 128.0 GB, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per vCPU. The processor includes a 260 MB L3 cache, 2 MB L2 cache, and 48 KB L1d cache, alongside hardware support for Advanced Vector Extensions (AVX-512) and Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX). No local storage is bundled with this instance, though it is Premium Storage capable, and it does not include any GPUs or complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

In benchmark evaluations, the D64nls_v6 demonstrates strong performance across multiple domains. The Intel Xeon processor delivers strong results in PassMark CPU tests, including extended instructions, encryption, and physics calculations. Memory bandwidth is a notable strength, with read-only and write operations for large blocks (256MB) scoring in the top 25%, and small-block cached reads showing strong throughput. Multi-threaded decompression via bzip3 and single-threaded gzip compression also rank in the top 25%. For web workloads, the instance achieves top-tier static web serving throughput, placing in the top 10% of tested systems. Database operations, evaluated via PassMark database tests and Redis SET operations, also show strong performance. In LLM inference benchmarks, the server exhibits top-tier prompt processing speeds for small models and strong prompt processing for medium and large models, though text generation speeds for small and large models remain in the average tier.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the D64nls_v6 offers a specialized hardware profile that trades memory density for compute power and network optimization. By reducing the RAM-to-vCPU ratio to 2.0 GB per core, it provides a cost-effective option for workloads that require high compute capacity and fast memory bandwidth without the overhead of large memory footprints. This resource balance makes the instance suitable for network-optimized applications, static web serving, high-throughput database operations, and compute-heavy tasks such as cryptography, compression, and prompt-heavy LLM inference.
description: Microsoft Azure D64nls_v6 is a network-optimized, low-memory virtual machine in the Dnlv6 family, powered by a 64-vCPU Intel Xeon 8573C processor with 128.0 GB of RAM. Operating on the x86_64 architecture under Microsoft Hyper-V, this instance features 32 physical cores with dedicated allocation and a 260 MB L3 cache. It lacks local storage and GPUs but is Premium Storage capable. Benchmarks reveal strong memory bandwidth, high-throughput static web serving, and strong performance in database operations, cryptography, and LLM prompt processing. With a low memory-to-vCPU ratio of 2.0 GB per core, the D64nls_v6 is a cost-efficient solution for compute-intensive and network-heavy workloads that do not require massive memory footprints, such as web serving, compression, and database tasks.
og_description: Microsoft Azure D64nls_v6 is a network-optimized virtual machine featuring 64 dedicated Intel Xeon vCPUs, 128.0 GB of RAM, and strong performance in web serving, database operations, and LLM prompt processing.
meta_description: Microsoft Azure D64nls_v6 is a network-optimized virtual machine with 64 dedicated Intel Xeon vCPUs, 128.0 GB of RAM, and no local storage.
tagline: A network-optimized, compute-heavy virtual machine featuring 64 dedicated Intel Xeon vCPUs and high-throughput memory bandwidth for efficient processing.
bullet_points: 64 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8573C processor with AMX and AVX-512 support.
128.0 GB of RAM providing a compute-focused 2.0 GB per vCPU ratio.
Strong memory bandwidth and top-tier static web serving throughput.
Premium Storage capable with no local storage or GPUs bundled.
Fits network-intensive workloads, database operations, and LLM prompt processing.
